CHRISTCHURCH HITCHIN
TRUSTEES’’’’ ANNUAL REPORT
and
STATEMENT OF FINANCIAL ACTIVITY
Year to 31 August 2014
2
Trustees’’’’ Report 2013-14
Trustees have the duty and responsibility to report on the activities of the Charity that
is Christchurch (Methodist and United Reformed) Church, Hitchin located at Bedford
Road, Hitchin, SG5 1HF. It is a registered charity, no. 1142830, and its constitution
was formally approved in 2010. Correspondence can be addressed to the Church
Secretary at that address.
The Trustees consist of the Minister, the Deacon and 13 persons elected from and by
the church’s members in accordance with the terms of the constitution for a fixed
term. Those serving throughout the year 2013-14 were as follows:
Rev. John Hardaker, Deacon Sheralee Devitt, Sara Crowley, Shirley Rossall, Chris
Blackman, Peter Ward, Sue Covington, Jeremy Rackham, Rob Bryan, David Rossall
Retired during the year:
Nora Evans, Sue Chastney, Katherine Harris, Bernard Higgs, Robin Meyers
Elected during the year:
Christine Warren, Anne Blackman, Mandy Pye
A vacancy for Steward remained unfilled during the year.
The Trustees, normally called the Leadership Group, meet at least 5 times each
year, and have overall responsibility for all aspects of Church Life.
Objectives and Activities
The Trustees are committed to develop the work of the charity, to strengthen the
discipleship of its members and to support and encourage others to develop their
faith and make a commitment as a follower of Christ.
The Church’s Mission Statement is: Led by the Spirit – Working in the Community
– Bringing people to Christ.
The Purpose of the Charity, as set out in the Christchurch constitution, is to advance
the Christian faith in the Area of Benefit in accordance with the principles and
practices of the Methodist and United Reformed Churches.
The Trustees have a duty under the constitution to give the necessary direction to
achieve its Purpose, and to:
“engage in a range of activities, either on its own or with others, including (but not
restricted to):
a. the celebration of public worship;
b. the teaching of the Christian faith;
c. mission and evangelism;
d. pastoral work, including visiting the sick and the bereaved;
e. the provision of facilities with a Christian ethos for the local community,
including (but not restricted to) the elderly, the young and other groups with
special needs; and
f. the support of other charities in the UK and overseas. ”
3
The Trustees wish to report that they have endeavoured to comply with the Church’s
Purpose as described below:
(a) Celebration of public worship
Worship is a key activity of any church to enable people to express praise and
thanks, to draw closer to God, and be open to the work of the Holy Spirit. We worship
privately and corporately, in small and large groups. We celebrate God’s presence in
the world and seek to magnify that presence with our lives. For many this provides an
essential foundation for all other activities.
Gathered worship at Christchurch has great variety. We provide summaries of the
essence of each planned worship time to provide guidance to those leading and
attending. Our time beginning at 10.45 a.m. on Sundays is designed to be diverse
and ‘Worship for All’. We have fewer evening acts of worship than in previous years,
as this seems to be a time best suited for occasional or special events. So we have a
monthly Traditional Worship that includes Holy Communion) and also share with
others in our town or region. This year, for a season, we held a weekly ‘Beta’ series
that included small discussion groups. Our weekly 9 a.m. ‘Traditional Worship’ is
short and focused, followed by a simple breakfast. Once a month it incorporates Holy
Communion with a more substantial breakfast. During Advent, we devised our own
themed services. We celebrated our 45th
Anniversary over a glorious weekend in
June that included special acts of worship. We also support worship in the middle of
the day monthly on Thursdays.
Our children and young people additionally enjoy their own age-appropriate activities
each Sunday morning. Worship is normally led by our minister, by other ministers
from the Methodist and URC churches, or by (lay) local preachers, sometimes with
the assistance of worship leaders.
There are many who contribute to our gathered worship, through playing the organ or
instruments in the band, or singing; by preparing and projecting words or images and
by providing amplification including a system for use with hearing aids loop; by
reading passages of Scripture or leading our prayers; using drama or puppets to
present the Scriptures or a message, and by arranging fresh flowers and creating
wall decorations. We also recognise the important contribution of all those who
welcome people into the building and into our acts of worship, and provide a clean,
comfortable and uncluttered building in which to worship.
(b) Teaching and Discipleship
The church is, at its heart, a community of people who have been called together as
agents of God’s healing and transforming love, professing the good news that Jesus
is Lord. It is a group of believers (and explorers) who gather to worship and to
encourage each other in the daily living out of their faith – loving and serving God
through loving and serving others. Each follower of Christ needs to work out how to
do this within their own context, a process which encompasses a lifetime, and so it is
of vital importance to offer teaching and practical support in growing as disciples.
The main focus of teaching at Christchurch currently comes within our Sunday
morning services and Junior Church sessions. The church asks its preachers to
follow the Lectionary plan, so that there is a structured exploration of all parts of the
Bible. As mentioned above, during Advent Christchurch devised its own themed
services and in autumn 2013 we ran a "whole-church Alpha" course, and 60 adult
and youth members and others took part. On the spring of 2014, we followed this up
with a "Beta" series looking at specific issues.
4
There are currently eleven house groups for adults, involving approximately 100
people, who meet regularly to deepen their faith and demonstrate love for God, each
other and for the world. Each house group has its own distinctive way of pursuing
discipleship. Many house groups read the Bible together, to discuss and to challenge
the participants to work out this teaching in practical ways. Others meet to encourage
faith development and/or its practical expressions by studying Christian books,
undertaking short courses together or initiating various projects. Some house groups
meet weekly, some fortnightly, some monthly. All members are encouraged to join
such a group, because the church recognises the value of mutual support in this
learning. No-one can live out their Christian faith alone.
There is separate, age-specific, support for children and young people (see below)
House groups are just one expression of shared discipleship. There are other
groups, such as the Men’s Breakfast and informal mutual-accountability partnerships,
which develop discipleship in a slightly different way. Prayer, is vital too, and a
number of church groups meet to pray together in different ways and for different
needs.
The church is conscious of the wide spectrum of people who are a part of our
fellowship, and therefore strives to mentor, challenge and support in a range of ways
to meet the needs of all. In order to achieve our core purpose as a church, intentional
faith development will continue to be a major commitment for us.
Youth & Children's ministry
Discipleship is also the main aim of Youth & Children’s ministry; to educate young
people about the Bible, enable them to discover more about their faith and equip
them to put that into action in their lives.
Our vision for our Youth work is that we long to see every child, teenager and leader
seeking to grow more like Jesus by deepening their roots in Christ and bearing fruit in
their lives. We hope to see all these roots and fruit growing in the lives of leaders and
young people.
We seek to be part of a loving church community that grows:
ROOTS
FRUIT
•
A heart for the Gospel
•
Loving and serving our church
•
A passion for worship
•
Reaching out in love
•
A whole life faith
•
Living 24/7 for Jesus
•
A desire to dig deeper into the bible
•
Putting others first
Christchurch has a full time Youth & Children’s leader who is supported by a strong
and large network of volunteers that enable us to deliver our goal vision. We arrange
our own training for volunteers and also have access to training events run by groups
outside Christchurch. We ensure that all volunteers have the appropriate
safeguarding checks in place.
Each week around 60 young people come along to age-specific groups at Junior
Church on Sunday mornings. The content of the sessions is usually based on one of
the lectionary Bible passages. A variety of methods are used to teach the stories,
discussions, dramatised readings, craft activities and songs all contribute to helping
the children learn and retain the information about the Bible.
For older teenagers there is also a more in-depth time of study and discussion on a
Sunday evening. We look at a key passage from the Bible each week and explore it
through conversation and prayer.
5
In order for Young people to be effectively discipled, it’s vital we make sure that what
they learn about the Bible is not limited to a set of stories but that what they hear
from the Bible also informs and guides them in their everyday lives. At Junior Church
we apply the concepts found in a biblical passage to their lives, but there are also
other opportunities for young people to learn about their faith through putting it into
practice.
The groups we run and support vary from traditional Sunday Junior church to music
and puppet groups, Sunday evening youth club, Parent and pre-school groups and
uniformed organisations from Beavers and Rainbows, through Brownies and Cubs
up to Scouts and Guides. We continue to look at different ways to engage with and
involve our children and young people in the life of Christchurch. We run a mentoring
programme for our young people to give them support and role models as they
develop in their faith.
Over the course of the year we run a number of special events. In the autumn we run
a back-to-school family fun day which enables families (churched or not) to spend an
afternoon relaxing together and building community. We also run a short residential
for our students; from summer 2013, we have had 12 young people away at
university, and this is an opportunity for us to equip them for the challenges they face
and for them to encourage each other.
In the spring we have our Big Weekend youth residential, where the secondary
school groups spend a weekend having fun and talking about Jesus together; we
also try to enable the young people themselves to take a lead in organising this
event. In the summer we take a group of teenagers to Soul Survivor. Also in the
summer, we run a very popular week long holiday club for 5-11s, and support a
holiday week on the Isle of Wight (which is run by a group of churches from the town
and their schools work team, Phase). All of these events require a team of committed
leaders, who are guided, encouraged and enabled by Tom.
(c) Mission and Outreach
Mission and Outreach initiatives and support are under three different headings -
Faith Sharing, Home and Overseas. Each section has a co-ordinator who has an
overview and supports the work and activities carried out by many different people.
Faith Sharing includes Street Angels, where the Christian faith is shared with those
who need support on the streets of our town on a Saturday night; and Alpha courses
-
helping people to learn more about the Christian faith. We also support special
projects where family members of our church are involved. At the present time, we
are supporting the work of Jon and Lisa Short, who are working with GBU 'Los
Grupos Biblicos Universitarios' in Gran Canaria.
Home covers those community activities which take place in our building [see details
below about Open Church, Lunch Club and Computer Club]. An Art space group is
held weekly, and is enjoyed by those for whom life is particularly challenging; these
people also benefit from the Bancroft [Sunday Lunch] project. We also welcome
Parents and Toddlers to various groups and visitors through our weekly Saturday
coffee mornings. The church continues to support, in practical and financial ways,
local initiatives, such as Simpsons Almshouses, North Herts Sanctuary for the
Homeless, and Meeting Needs, which especially helps those setting up home on a
limited income. The regular prayer station outside Christchurch offers support in a
different way, by praying through people's prayer requests for them, and offering a
listening ear if needed. Christchurch has been involved in supporting and providing
for the local Food Bank. A new initiative has been the monthly Coffee+ on a
Saturday, which provides a family-friendly place for all ages to meet.
6
Prayer and financial commitment continue to be very much key features of our
commitment to serving people overseas. The church continues its support of the
Lideta community development project in Ethiopia through the charity World Vision. It
is also committed to pray for and financially support brothers and sisters in Christ
who face persecution in countries throughout the world through charities such as
Open Doors and Barnabas Fund. We also continue to respond throughout the year to
emergency appeals as a result of either man-made or natural disasters. A regular
Traidcraft stall is held at church at least once a month, promoting awareness of the
need for fair and equal trade with developing countries. Through MECO, we offer
support to the persecuted church.
(d) Pastoral work
Christchurch endeavours to offer a comprehensive pastoral support network to all
who have connections with Christchurch. Pastoral care, including visiting the sick and
bereaved, is led and overseen by the Ministry Team who in turn involve area and
individual pastoral visitors. House groups and other organizations that are under the
Christchurch umbrella also offer pastoral care and support. Pastoral care is offered to
those at Archers Court, Elmside and at The Cedars, properties associated with MHA
(formerly Methodist Homes for the Aged).
Open church, prayer station and outreach activities within the local community
provide companionship to those who wish to talk, be prayed for or be listened to.
Individual support is available to the bereaved.
Safeguarding is based on the Methodist Church policy “Safeguarding Children,
Young People and Vulnerable Adults”. Our safeguarding group is responsible for
implementing this policy and ensuring that all users of our premises are aware of the
policy and its implications.
(e) Provision of facilities with a Christian ethos for the local community
Facilities with a Christian ethos provided for the local community include Open
Church: on 4 weekdays the church is open between 10.00 and 15.00, offering
hot/cold drinks and biscuits, as well as a listening ear and a quiet place to pray. It is
much appreciated by the homeless and many others.
There is a weekly Lunch Club; around 30 elderly people (referred by Social Services
or Age UK) are transported to the church, the majority by minibus, and provided with
activities and hospitality as well as lunch.
A Computer Centre is staffed by church members on 2 days per week offering
opportunity for anybody wishing to learn and practise skills at operating a computer.
The ‘After School Club’, run weekly by the Youth Worker and local Christian Schools’
Worker, offers to youngsters a range of activities and a place to chat.
(f) Support of other charities in the UK and overseas.
During the year a total of over £14,000 was donated to some 30 other charities in the
UK and places abroad including Syria and the Philippines as well as other charities
working worldwide. £3,000 was donated from the church’s general account, but the
bulk of the charitable giving was raised through donations and a variety of methods
including coffee mornings, concerts, charity quizzes, social activities and sponsored
events, organised by individuals and Groups associated with the church.
7
Activities shared with others
Christchurch also engages in a range of activities with others including the North
Herts Circuit of the Methodist church, the North Herts Group of United Reformed
Churches, and with Letchworth Free Church with whom it shares a minister.
Christchurch plays a prominent role in Churches Together in Hitchin which organises
events such as a Good Friday Walk of Witness, and communication of information
throughout the town about Christmas and Easter services at all constituent churches,
and also co-ordinates the annual house-to–house collection for Christian Aid.
With other churches in the town Christchurch, supports other community-led
initiatives including the North Herts Sanctuary and associated charities, helping the
Homeless and those then moving into accommodation.
More detail on the activities of the Charity can be found in the Christchurch Annual
Report, which is available on request.
Some statistics
Christchurch had 266 members as at 31 August 2014.
Average attendance at all services on a Sunday was 194, as measured during the
month of October 2012, in accordance with the procedures of the Methodist church.
This average consisted of 162 adults and 32 under 19s.
Maintenance of the Premises
Maintenance and cleaning of the premises at Christchurch are carried out and
monitored by the Finance & Property Committee and the Property Co-ordinator. A
Property Schedule has been prepared for forwarding to the Methodist
Superintendent, in accordance with Methodist Church procedures.
Apart from the completion of programmes already in place, no major works were
initiated in 2013-14 other than renewal of heating equipment. Some interior lighting
has been replaced in a number of rooms. Plans are also in place for painting of the
exterior timber and some classrooms, and for other electrical works.
During the past twelve months, approximately £40,000 has been spent on cleaning,
maintenance and repairs.
Risk Assessment
Each year, the Trustees carry out a Risk Assessment covering a range of risks,
including fire, accidents and other Health & Safety matters, finance and staff
shortage. Follow-up action is initiated as necessary.
Accounts
The Treasurer has prepared accounts for the year 2013-14, which have been
approved, have been audited by an Independent Examiner, and are attached (9
pages) to this report as submitted to the Methodist Church.
The Trustees have also formulated Accounting Policies and a Reserves Policy and
these are set out below:
8
Accounting policies
Incoming Resources
These are included in the Statement of Financial Activities (SOFA) when the church
becomes entitled to the resources, the Trustees are virtually certain they will receive
the resources and the monetary value can be measured with sufficient reliability.
Resources expended
Expenditure is recognised when a liability is incurred, or a constructive obligation
arises that results in the payment being unavoidable.
Liability recognition
Liabilities are recognized as soon as there is a legal or constructive obligation
committing the church to payout resources. Future income is not anticipated (in
accordance with SORP 2005).
Tangible fixed assets for use by church
Tangible fixed assets are capitalised if they can be used for more than one year, and
cost at least £1000. They are valued at cost or a reasonable value on receipt. Where
cost is not available, the Trustees have provided a reasonable estimate of cost or of
the buildings' current value to the church.
Reserves policy
It is the Trustees’ policy to maintain a balance in reserve which equates to 6 months
of the assessments due to the Methodist and United Reformed Churches, and wages
for the Cleaner and Youth Worker, plus potential employment termination costs and
redundancy liabilities. In addition provision has been made for capital projects
expected to be carried out during the year.
Further details about the reserves policy are provided in the 2-page attachment
following the statement of accounts.
Approved at the Meeting of the Leadership Group on 23 June 2015, and signed on
behalf of the Trustees:
David Rossall

receivable and any gains or losses on revaluation at the year end are shown in the SOFA.

with Statement of Recommended Practice (SORP 2005) and the Charities Act 2011. There has been no change to the accounting
policies since last year. The format of the SOFA balance sheet takes advantage of the small charity concessions set out in the
SORP.
These are included in the Statement of Financial Activities (SOFA) when the church becomes entitled to the resources, the trustees
are virtually certain they will receive the resources, and the monetary value can be measured with sufficient reliability.

a reasonable value on receipt. Where cost is not available, the Church Council may provide a reasonable estimate of cost or of the

on demolition and reinstating the current building and not the original cost less accumulated depreciation. A professional valuation is
not required, but if available use that figure. Provision is made for depreciation where appropriate.

income has not been anticipated (SORP 2005).

TRUSTEES’ ANNUAL REPORT
and
STATEMENT OF FINANCIAL ACTIVITY
Year to 31 August 2014
Trustees’ Report 2013-14
Trustees have the duty and responsibility to report on the activities of the Charity that
is Christchurch (Methodist and United Reformed) Church, Hitchin located at Bedford
Road, Hitchin, SG5 1HF. It is a registered charity, no. 1142830, and its constitution
was formally approved in 2010. Correspondence can be addressed to the Church
Secretary at that address.
The Trustees consist of the Minister, the Deacon and 13 persons elected from and by
the church’s members in accordance with the terms of the constitution for a fixed
term. Those serving throughout the year 2013-14 were as follows:
Rev. John Hardaker, Deacon Sheralee Devitt, Sara Crowley, Shirley Rossall, Chris
Blackman, Peter Ward, Sue Covington, Jeremy Rackham, Rob Bryan, David Rossall
Retired during the year:
Nora Evans, Sue Chastney, Katherine Harris, Bernard Higgs, Robin Meyers
Elected during the year:
Christine Warren, Anne Blackman, Mandy Pye
A vacancy for Steward remained unfilled during the year.
The Trustees, normally called the Leadership Group, meet at least 5 times each
year, and have overall responsibility for all aspects of Church Life.
Objectives and Activities
The Trustees are committed to develop the work of the charity, to strengthen the
discipleship of its members and to support and encourage others to develop their
faith and make a commitment as a follower of Christ.
The Church’s Mission Statement is: Led by the Spirit — Working in the Community
— Bringing people to Christ.
The Purpose of the Charity, as set out in the Christchurch constitution, is to advance
the Christian faith in the Area of Benefit in accordance with the principles and
practices of the Methodist and United Reformed Churches.
The Trustees have a duty under the constitution to give the necessary direction to
achieve its Purpose, and to:
“‘engage in a range of activities, either on its own or with others, including (but not
restricted to):
a. the celebration of public worship;
the teaching of the Christian faith;
mission and evangelism;
pastoral work, including visiting the sick and the bereaved;
the provision of facilities with a Christian ethos for the local community,
including (but not restricted to) the elderly, the young and other groups with
special needs; and
©ao o
f. the support of other charities in the UK and overseas. ”
The Trustees wish to report that they have endeavoured to comply with the Church’s
Purpose as described below:
(a) Celebration of public worship
Worship is a key activity of any church to enable people to express praise and
thanks, to draw closer to God, and be open to the work of the Holy Spirit. We worship
privately and corporately, in small and large groups. We celebrate God’s presence in
the world and seek to magnify that presence with our lives. For many this provides an
essential foundation for all other activities.
Gathered worship at Christchurch has great variety. We provide summaries of the
essence of each planned worship time to provide guidance to those leading and
attending. Our time beginning at 10.45 a.m. on Sundays is designed to be diverse
and ‘Worship for All'. We have fewer evening acts of worship than in previous years,
as this seems to be a time best suited for occasional or special events. So we have a
monthly Traditional Worship that includes Holy Communion) and also share with
others in our town or region. This year, for a season, we held a weekly ‘Beta’ series
that included small discussion groups. Our weekly 9 a.m. ‘Traditional Worship’ is
short and focused, followed by a simple breakfast. Once a month it incorporates Holy
Communion with a more substantial breakfast. During Advent, we devised our own
themed services. We celebrated our 45™ Anniversary over a glorious weekend in
June that included special acts of worship. We also support worship in the middle of
the day monthly on Thursdays.
Our children and young people additionally enjoy their own age-appropriate activities
each Sunday morning. Worship is normally led by our minister, by other ministers
from the Methodist and URC churches, or by (lay) local preachers, sometimes with
the assistance of worship leaders.
There are many who contribute to our gathered worship, through playing the organ or
instruments in the band, or singing; by preparing and projecting words or images and
by providing amplification including a system for use with hearing aids loop; by
reading passages of Scripture or leading our prayers; using drama or puppets to
present the Scriptures or a message, and by arranging fresh flowers and creating
wall decorations. We also recognise the important contribution of all those who
welcome people into the building and into our acts of worship, and provide a clean,
comfortable and uncluttered building in which to worship.
(b) Teaching and Discipleship
The church is, at its heart, a community of people who have been called together as
agents of God’s healing and transforming love, professing the good news that Jesus
is Lord. It is a group of believers (and explorers) who gather to worship and to
encourage each other in the daily living out of their faith — loving and serving God
through loving and serving others. Each follower of Christ needs to work out how to
do this within their own context, a process which encompasses a lifetime, and so it is
of vital importance to offer teaching and practical support in growing as disciples.
The main focus of teaching at Christchurch currently comes within our Sunday
morning services and Junior Church sessions. The church asks its preachers to
follow the Lectionary plan, so that there is a structured exploration of all parts of the
Bible. As mentioned above, during Advent Christchurch devised its own themed
services and in autumn 2013 we ran a "whole-church Alpha" course, and 60 adult
and youth members and others took part. On the spring of 2014, we followed this up
with a "Beta" series looking at specific issues.
There are currently eleven house groups for adults, involving approximately 100
people, who meet regularly to deepen their faith and demonstrate love for God, each
other and for the world. Each house group has its own distinctive way of pursuing
discipleship. Many house groups read the Bible together, to discuss and to challenge
the participants to work out this teaching in practical ways. Others meet to encourage
faith development and/or its practical expressions by studying Christian books,
undertaking short courses together or initiating various projects. Some house groups
meet weekly, some fortnightly, some monthly. All members are encouraged to join
such a group, because the church recognises the value of mutual support in this
learning. No-one can live out their Christian faith alone.
There is separate, age-specific, support for children and young people (see below)
House groups are just one expression of shared discipleship. There are other
groups, such as the Men’s Breakfast and informal mutual-accountability partnerships,
which develop discipleship in a slightly different way. Prayer, is vital too, and a
number of church groups meet to pray together in different ways and for different
needs.
The church is conscious of the wide spectrum of people who are a part of our
fellowship, and therefore strives to mentor, challenge and support in a range of ways
to meet the needs of all. In order to achieve our core purpose as a church, intentional
faith development will continue to be a major commitment for us.
Youth & Children's ministry
Discipleship is also the main aim of Youth & Children’s ministry; to educate young
people about the Bible, enable them to discover more about their faith and equip
them to put that into action in their lives.
Our vision for our Youth work is that we long to see every child, teenager and leader
seeking to grow more like Jesus by deepening their roots in Christ and bearing fruit in
their lives. We hope to see all these roots and fruit growing in the lives of leaders and
young people.
We seek to be part of a loving church community that grows:
ROOTS FRUIT
e A heart for the Gospel e Loving and serving our church
e A passion for worship Reaching out in love
e A whole life faith Living 24/7 for Jesus
e A desire to dig deeper into the bible Putting others first
Christchurch has a full time Youth & Children’s leader who is supported by a strong
and large network of volunteers that enable us to deliver our goal vision. We arrange
our own training for volunteers and also have access to training events run by groups
outside Christchurch. We ensure that all volunteers have the appropriate
safeguarding checks in place.
Each week around 60 young people come along to age-specific groups at Junior
Church on Sunday mornings. The content of the sessions is usually based on one of
the lectionary Bible passages. A variety of methods are used to teach the stories,
discussions, dramatised readings, craft activities and songs all contribute to helping
the children learn and retain the information about the Bible.
For older teenagers there is also a more in-depth time of study and discussion on a
Sunday evening. We look at a key passage from the Bible each week and explore it
through conversation and prayer.
In order for Young people to be effectively discipled, it's vital we make sure that what
they learn about the Bible is not limited to a set of stories but that what they hear
from the Bible also informs and guides them in their everyday lives. At Junior Church
we apply the concepts found in a biblical passage to their lives, but there are also
other opportunities for young people to learn about their faith through putting it into
practice.
The groups we run and support vary from traditional Sunday Junior church to music
and puppet groups, Sunday evening youth club, Parent and pre-school groups and
uniformed organisations from Beavers and Rainbows, through Brownies and Cubs
up to Scouts and Guides. We continue to look at different ways to engage with and
involve our children and young people in the life of Christchurch. We run a mentoring
programme for our young people to give them support and role models as they
develop in their faith.
Over the course of the year we run a number of special events. In the autumn we run
a back-to-school family fun day which enables families (churched or not) to spend an
afternoon relaxing together and building community. We also run a short residential
for our students; from summer 2013, we have had 12 young people away at
university, and this is an opportunity for us to equip them for the challenges they face
and for them to encourage each other.
In the spring we have our Big Weekend youth residential, where the secondary
school groups spend a weekend having fun and talking about Jesus together; we
also try to enable the young people themselves to take a lead in organising this
event. In the summer we take a group of teenagers to Soul Survivor. Also in the
summer, we run a very popular week long holiday club for 5-11s, and support a
holiday week on the Isle of Wight (which is run by a group of churches from the town
and their schools work team, Phase). All of these events require a team of committed
leaders, who are guided, encouraged and enabled by Tom.
(c) Mission and Outreach
Mission and Outreach initiatives and support are under three different headings -
Faith Sharing, Home and Overseas. Each section has a co-ordinator who has an
overview and supports the work and activities carried out by many different people.
Faith Sharing includes Street Angels, where the Christian faith is shared with those
who need support on the streets of our town on a Saturday night; and Alpha courses
- helping people to learn more about the Christian faith. We also support special
projects where family members of our church are involved. At the present time, we
are supporting the work of Jon and Lisa Short, who are working with GBU 'Los
Grupos Biblicos Universitarios' in Gran Canaria.
Home covers those community activities which take place in our building [see details
below about Open Church, Lunch Club and Computer Club]. An Art space group is
held weekly, and is enjoyed by those for whom life is particularly challenging; these
people also benefit from the Bancroft [Sunday Lunch] project. We also welcome
Parents and Toddlers to various groups and visitors through our weekly Saturday
coffee mornings. The church continues to support, in practical and financial ways,
local initiatives, such as Simpsons Almshouses, North Herts Sanctuary for the
Homeless, and Meeting Needs, which especially helps those setting up home on a
limited income. The regular prayer station outside Christchurch offers support in a
different way, by praying through people's prayer requests for them, and offering a
listening ear if needed. Christchurch has been involved in supporting and providing
for the local Food Bank. A new initiative has been the monthly Coffee+ on a
Saturday, which provides a family-friendly place for all ages to meet.
Prayer and financial commitment continue to be very much key features of our
commitment to serving people overseas. The church continues its support of the
Lideta community development project in Ethiopia through the charity World Vision. It
is also committed to pray for and financially support brothers and sisters in Christ
who face persecution in countries throughout the world through charities such as
Open Doors and Barnabas Fund. We also continue to respond throughout the year to
emergency appeals as a result of either man-made or natural disasters. A regular
Traidcraft stall is held at church at least once a month, promoting awareness of the
need for fair and equal trade with developing countries. Through MECO, we offer
support to the persecuted church.
(d) Pastoral work
Christchurch endeavours to offer a comprehensive pastoral support network to all
who have connections with Christchurch. Pastoral care, including visiting the sick and
bereaved, is led and overseen by the Ministry Team who in turn involve area and
individual pastoral visitors. House groups and other organizations that are under the
Christchurch umbrella also offer pastoral care and support. Pastoral care is offered to
those at Archers Court, EImside and at The Cedars, properties associated with MHA
(formerly Methodist Homes for the Aged).
Open church, prayer station and outreach activities within the local community
provide companionship to those who wish to talk, be prayed for or be listened to.
Individual support is available to the bereaved.
Safeguarding is based on the Methodist Church policy “Safeguarding Children,
Young People and Vulnerable Adults”. Our safeguarding group is responsible for
implementing this policy and ensuring that all users of our premises are aware of the
policy and its implications.
(e) Provision of facilities with a Christian ethos for the local community
Facilities with a Christian ethos provided for the local community include Open
Church: on 4 weekdays the church is open between 10.00 and 15.00, offering
hot/cold drinks and biscuits, as well as a listening ear and a quiet place to pray. It is
much appreciated by the homeless and many others.
There is a weekly Lunch Club; around 30 elderly people (referred by Social Services
or Age UK) are transported to the church, the majority by minibus, and provided with
activities and hospitality as well as lunch.
A Computer Centre is staffed by church members on 2 days per week offering
opportunity for anybody wishing to learn and practise skills at operating a computer.
The ‘After School Club’, run weekly by the Youth Worker and local Christian Schools’
Worker, offers to youngsters a range of activities and a place to chat.
(f) Support of other charities in the UK and overseas.
During the year a total of over £14,000 was donated to some 30 other charities in the
UK and places abroad including Syria and the Philippines as well as other charities
working worldwide. £3,000 was donated from the church’s general account, but the
bulk of the charitable giving was raised through donations and a variety of methods
including coffee mornings, concerts, charity quizzes, social activities and sponsored
events, organised by individuals and Groups associated with the church.
Activities shared with others
Christchurch also engages in a range of activities with others including the North
Herts Circuit of the Methodist church, the North Herts Group of United Reformed
Churches, and with Letchworth Free Church with whom it shares a minister.
Christchurch plays a prominent role in Churches Together in Hitchin which organises
events such as a Good Friday Walk of Witness, and communication of information
throughout the town about Christmas and Easter services at all constituent churches,
and also co-ordinates the annual house-to—house collection for Christian Aid.
With other churches in the town Christchurch, supports other community-led
initiatives including the North Herts Sanctuary and associated charities, helping the
Homeless and those then moving into accommodation.
More detail on the activities of the Charity can be found in the Christchurch Annual
Report, which is available on request.
Some statistics
Christchurch had 266 members as at 31 August 2014.
Average attendance at all services on a Sunday was 194, as measured during the
month of October 2012, in accordance with the procedures of the Methodist church.
This average consisted of 162 adults and 32 under 19s.
Maintenance of the Premises
Maintenance and cleaning of the premises at Christchurch are carried out and
monitored by the Finance & Property Committee and the Property Co-ordinator. A
Property Schedule has been prepared for forwarding to the Methodist
Superintendent, in accordance with Methodist Church procedures.
Apart from the completion of programmes already in place, no major works were
initiated in 2013-14 other than renewal of heating equipment. Some interior lighting
has been replaced in a number of rooms. Plans are also in place for painting of the
exterior timber and some classrooms, and for other electrical works.
During the past twelve months, approximately £40,000 has been spent on cleaning,
maintenance and repairs.
Risk Assessment
Each year, the Trustees carry out a Risk Assessment covering a range of risks,
including fire, accidents and other Health & Safety matters, finance and staff
shortage. Follow-up action is initiated as necessary.
Accounts
The Treasurer has prepared accounts for the year 2013-14, which have been
approved, have been audited by an Independent Examiner, and are attached (9
pages) to this report as submitted to the Methodist Church.
The Trustees have also formulated Accounting Policies and a Reserves Policy and
these are set out below:
Accounting policies
Incoming Resources
These are included in the Statement of Financial Activities (SOFA) when the church
becomes entitled to the resources, the Trustees are virtually certain they will receive
the resources and the monetary value can be measured with sufficient reliability.
Resources expended
Expenditure is recognised when a liability is incurred, or a constructive obligation
arises that results in the payment being unavoidable.
Liability recognition
Liabilities are recognized as soon as there is a legal or constructive obligation
committing the church to payout resources. Future income is not anticipated (in
accordance with SORP 2005).
Tangible fixed assets for use by church
Tangible fixed assets are capitalised if they can be used for more than one year, and
cost at least £1000. They are valued at cost or a reasonable value on receipt. Where
cost is not available, the Trustees have provided a reasonable estimate of cost or of
the buildings' current value to the church.
Reserves policy
It is the Trustees’ policy to maintain a balance in reserve which equates to 6 months
of the assessments due to the Methodist and United Reformed Churches, and wages
for the Cleaner and Youth Worker, plus potential employment termination costs and
redundancy liabilities. In addition provision has been made for capital projects
expected to be carried out during the year.
Further details about the reserves policy are provided in the 2-page attachment
following the statement of accounts.
Approved at the Meeting of the Leadership Group on 23 June 2015, and signed on
behalf of the Trustees:

Notes to the Accounts
1 Basis of accounting
These accounts have been prepared on the basis of historic cost (except that investments are shown at market value) in accordance
with Statement of Recommended Practice (SORP 2005) and the Charities Act 2011. There has been no change to the accounting
policies since last year. The format of the SOFA balance sheet takes advantage of the small charity concessions set out in the
SORP.
2 Funds
The funds held constitute: General Funds held for any purpose of the charity which are Unrestricted, Restricted funds which are held
for a narrower purpose and Endowment funds which represent gifts, the capital normally being unavailable for spending, and the
income from which is either Restricted or Unrestricted. Details of each material fund are disclosed in the final Note to these Accounts.
Any fund may be held in ways other than in cash.
3 Accounting policies
Incoming Resources
These are included in the Statement of Financial Activities (SOFA) when the church becomes entitled to the resources, the trustees
are virtually certain they will receive the resources, and the monetary value can be measured with sufficient reliability.
Resources Expended
Expenditure is recognised when a liability is incurred, or a constructive obligation arises that results in the payment being unavoidable.
Liability recognition
Liabilities are recognised as soon as there is a legal or constructive obligation committing the church to pay out resources. Future
income has not been anticipated (SORP 2005).
Tangible fixed assets for use by church
Tangible fixed assets are capitalised if they can be used for more than one year, and cost at least £1000. They are valued at cost or
a reasonable value on receipt. Where cost is not available, the Church Council may provide a reasonable estimate of cost or of the
buildings’ current value to the church. An estimate based on insurance value should take account of the fact that cover will be based
on demolition and reinstating the current building and not the original cost less accumulated depreciation. A professional valuation is
not required, but if available use that figure. Provision is made for depreciation where appropriate.
Investment properties
This includes Investment properties which are not used for the purposes of the charity (and never used for that purpose). A
professional valuation is not required, but if available use that figure. The insured value may be used.
Investments
Investments are valued in the balance sheet at market value at the year end. Investment income is included in the accounts when
receivable and any gains or losses on revaluation at the year end are shown in the SOFA.
Include investments held with the Central Finance Board. Do not include Deposit Funds, such as TIF (Trustees Interest Fund) held
via Trustees for Methodist Church Purposes. These latter funds should be shown separately.
Debtors and Prepayments
These amounts are owed to the Church and include payments made by the Church for services yet to be received.
Creditors
These amounts are owed by the Church and include any amounts accrued as payable this year but will be paid in future years.
RMO/Accruals Accounts/2011 page 4 of 9
Christchurch Methodist and United Reformed Church
Loans
If the Church has borrowed any money, please give the information in the designated section. Loans made to Churches
may be confidential and may be free of interest. It is not necessary to breach confidentiality in recording sources: use the
word ‘Individuals' if appropriate.
Endowment Funds
Where the Church holds money which cannot be spent but must be retained (as capital) to generate an income the
money or assets to be retained are known as an Endowment Fund. The capital should be shown in the appropriate
column in the Balance Sheet. The income generated may be restricted or unrestricted depending upon the terms of the
endowment.
Net current assets/liabilities
Deduct Creditors from Total current assets.
Total assets less net current liabilities
Deduct net current assets/liabilities from the total of fixed and current assets.
Net assets totals must agree with Total Funds held.

TRUSTEES' ANNUAL REPORT
and
STATEMENT OF FINANCIAL ACTIVITY
Year to 31 August 2014
1
Trustees' Report 2013-14
Trustees have the duty and responsibility to report on the activities of the Charity that
is Christchurch (Methodist and United Reformed) Church, Hitchin located at Bedford
Road, Hitchin, SG5 1HF. It is a registered charity, no. 1142830, and its constitution
was formally approved in 2010. Correspondence can be addressed to the Church
Secretary at that address.
The Trustees consist of the Minister, the Deacon and 13 persons elected from and by
the church's members in accordance with the terms of the constitution for a fixed
term. Those serving throughout the year 2013-14 were as follows:
Rev. J ohn Hardaker, Deacon Sheralee Devitt, Sara Crowley, Shirley Rossall, Chris
Blackman, Peter Ward, Sue Covington, J eremy Rackham, Rob Bryan, David Rossall
Retired during the year:
Nora Evans, Sue Chastney, Katherine Harris, Bernard Higgs, Robin Meyers
Elected during the year:
Christine Warren, Anne Blackman, Mandy Pye
A vacancy for Steward remained unfilled during the year.
The Trustees, normally called the Leadership Group, meet at least 5 times each
year, and have overall responsibility for all aspects of Church Life.
Objectives and Activities
The Trustees are committed to develop the work of the charity, to strengthen the
discipleship of its members and to support and encourage others to develop their
faith and make a commitment as a follower of Christ.
The Church's Mission Statement is: Led by the Spirit Working in the Community
Bringing people to Christ.
The Purpose of the Charity, as set out in the Christchurch constitution, is to advance
the Christian faith in the Area of Benefit in accordance with the principles and
practices of the Methodist and United Reformed Churches.
The Trustees have a duty under the constitution to give the necessary direction to
achieve its Purpose, and to:
"engage in a range of activities, either on its own or with others, including (but not
restricted to):
a. the celebration of public worship;
b. the teaching of the Christian faith;
C. mission and evangelism;
d. pastoral work, including visiting the sick and the bereaved;
e. the provision of facilities with a Christian ethos for the local community,
including (but not restricted to) the elderly, the young and other groups with
special needs; and
f.
the support of other charities in the UK and overseas."
2
The Trustees wish to report that they have endeavoured to comply with the Church's
Purpose as described below:
(a) Celebration of public worship
Worship is a key activity of any church to enable people to express praise and
thanks, to draw closer to God, and be open to the work of the Holy Spirit. We worship
privately and corporately, in small and large groups. We celebrate God's presence in
the world and seek to magnify that presence with our lives. For many this provides an
essential foundation for all other activities.
Gathered worship at Christchurch has great variety. We provide summaries of the
essence of each planned worship time to provide guidance to those leading and
attending. Our time beginning at 10.45 a.m. on Sundays is designed to be diverse
and 'Worship for All'. We have fewer evening acts of worship than in previous years,
as this seems to be a time best suited for occasional or special events. So we have
a
monthly Traditional Worship that includes Holy Communion) and also share with
others in our town or region. This year, for a season, we held a weekly 'Beta' series
that included small discussion groups. Our weekly 9 a.m. Traditional Worship' is
short and focused, followed by a simple breakfast Once a month it incorporates Holy
Communion with a more substantial breakfast During Advent, we devised our own
themed services. We celebrated our 45th Anniversary over a glorious weekend in
J une that included special acts of worship. We also support worship in the middle of
the day monthly on Thursdays.
Our children and young people additionally enjoy their own age-appropriate activities
each Sunday morning. Worship is normally led by our minister, by other ministers
from the Methodist and URC churches, or by (lay) local preachers, sometimes with
the assistance of worship leaders.
There are many who contribute to our gathered worship, through playing the organ or
instruments in the band, or singing; by preparing and projecting words or images and
by providing amplification including a system for use with hearing aids loop; by
reading passages of Scripture or leading our prayers; using drama or puppets to
present the Scriptures or a message, and by arranging fresh flowers and creating
wall decorations. We also recognise the important contribution of all those who
welcome people into the building and into our acts of worship, and provide a clean,
comfortable and uncluttered building in which to worship.
(b) Teaching and Discipleship
The church is, at its heart, a community of people who have been called together as
agents of God's healing and transforming love, professing the good news that J esus
is Lord. It is a group of believers (and explorers) who gather to worship and to
encourage each other in the daily living out of their faith loving and serving God
through loving and serving others. Each follower of Christ needs to work out how to
do this within their own context, a process which encompasses a lifetime, and so it is
of vital importance to offer teaching and practical support in growing as disciples.
The main focus of teaching at Christchurch currently comes within our Sunday
morning services and J unior Church sessions. The church asks its preachers to
follow the Lectionary plan, so that there is a structured exploration of all parts of the
Bible. As mentioned above, during Advent Christchurch devised its own themed
services and in autumn 2013 we ran a "whole-church Alpha" course, and 60 adult
and youth members and others took part. On the spring of 2014, we followed this up
with a "Beta" series looking at specific issues.
3
There are currently eleven house groups for adults, involving approximately 100
people, who meet regularly to deepen their faith and demonstrate love for God, each
other and for the world. Each house group has its own distinctive way of pursuing
discipleship. Many house groups read the Bible together, to discuss and to challenge
the participants to work out this teaching in practical ways. Others meet to encourage
faith development and/or its practical expressions by studying Christian books,
undertaking short courses together or initiating various projects. Some house groups
meet weekly, some fortnightly, some monthly. All members are encouraged to join
such a group, because the church recognises the value of mutual support in this
learning. No-one can live out their Christian faith alone.
There is separate, age-specific, support for children and young people (see below)
House groups are just one expression of shared discipleship. There are other
groups, such as the Men's Breakfast and informal mutual-accountability partnerships,
which develop discipleship in a slightly different way. Prayer, is vital too, and a
number of church groups meet to pray together in different ways and for different
needs.
The church is conscious of the wide spectrum of people who are a part of our
fellowship, and therefore strives to mentor, challenge and support in a range of ways
to meet the needs of all. In order to achieve our core purpose as a church, intentional
faith development will continue to be a major commitment for us.
Youth & Children's ministry
Discipleship is also the main aim of Youth & Children's ministry; to educate young
people about the Bible, enable them to discover more about their faith and equip
them to put that into action in their lives.
Our vision for our Youth work is that we long to see every child, teenager and leader
seeking to grow more like JJ esus by deepening their roots in Christ and bearing fruit in
their lives. We hope to see all these roots and fruit growing in the lives of leaders and
young people.
We seek to be part of a loving church community that grows:
ROOTS
FRUIT
A heart for the Gospel
Loving and serving our church
A passion for worship
Reaching out in love
A whole life faith
Living 24/7 for J esus
A desire to dig deeper into the bible
Putting others first
Christchurch has a full time Youth & Children's leader who is supported by a strong
and large network of volunteers that enable us to deliver our goal vision. We arrange
our own training for volunteers and also have access to training events run by groups
outside Christchurch. We ensure that all volunteers have the appropriate
safeguarding checks in place.
Each week around 60 young people come along to age-specific groups at J unior
Church on Sunday mornings. The content of the sessions is usually based on one of
the lectionary Bible passages. A variety of methods are used to teach the stories,
discussions, dramatised readings, craft activities and songs all contribute to helping
the children learn and retain the information about the Bible.
For older teenagers there is also a more in-depth time of study and discussion on a
Sunday evening. We look at a key passage from the Bible each week and explore it
through conversation and prayer.
4
In order for Young people to be effectively discipled, it's vital we make sure that what
they learn about the Bible is not limited to a set of stories but that what they hear
from the Bible also informs and guides them in their everyday lives At unior Church
we apply the concepts found in a biblica passage to their lives, but there are also
other opportunities for young people to learn about their faith through putting it into
practice.
The groups we run and support vary from traditiona Sunday J unior church to music
and puppet groups, Sunday evening youth club, Parent and pre-school groups and
uniformed organisations from Beavers and Rainbows, through Brownies and Cubs
up to Scouts and Guides. We continue to look at different ways to engage with and
involve our children and young people in the life of Christchurch. We run a mentoring
programme for our young people to give them support and role models as they
develop in their faith.
Over the course of the year we run a number of special events. In the autumn we run
a back-to-school family fun day which enables families (churched or not) to spend an
afternoon relaxing together and building community. We also run a short residential
for our students; from summer 2013, we have had 12 young people away at
university, and this is an opportunity for us to equip them for the challenges they face
and for them to encourage each other.
In the spring we have our Big Weekend youth residential, where the secondary
school groups spend a weekend having fun and talking about esus together; we
also try to enable the young people themselves to take a lead in organising this
event. In the summer we take a group of teenagers to Soul Survivor. Also in the
summer, we run a very popular week long holiday club for 5-11s, and support
a
holiday week on the Isle of Wight (which is run by a group of churches from the town
and their schools work team, Phase). All of these events require a team of committed
leaders, who are guided, encouraged and enabled by Tom.
(c) Mission and Outreach
Mission and Outreach initiatives and support are under three different headings
Faith Sharing, Home and Overseas. Each section has a co-ordinator who has an
overview and supports the work and activities carried out by many different people.
Faith Sharing includes Street Angels, where the Christian faith is shared with those
who need support on the streets of our town on a Saturday night; and Alpha courses
helping people to learn more about the Christian faith. We also support special
projects where family members of our church are involved. At the present time, we
are supporting the work of J on and Lisa Short, who are working with GBU 'Los
Grupos Biblicos Universitarios' in Gran Canaria.
Home covers those community activities which take place in our building [see details
below about Open Church, Lunch Club and Computer Club]. An Art space group is
held weekly, and is enjoyed by those for whom life is particularly challenging; these
people also benefit from the Bancroft Sunday Lunch] project. We also welcome
Parents and Toddlers to various groups and visitors through our weekly Saturday
coffee mornings. The church continues to support, in practica and financial ways,
local initiatives, such as Simpsons Almshouses, North Herts Sanctuary for the
Homeless, and Meeting Needs, which especially helps those setting up home on a
limited income. The regular prayer station outside Christchurch offers support in a
different way, by praying through people's prayer requests for them, and offering a
listening ear if needed. Christchurch has been involved in supporting and providing
for the local Food Bank. A new initiative has been the monthly Coffee+ on a
Saturday, which provides a family-friendly place for all ages to meet.
5
Prayer and financial commitment continue to be very much key features of our
commitment to serving people overseas. The church continues its support of the
Lideta community development project in Ethiopia through the charity World Vision. it
is also committed to pray for and financially support brothers and sisters in Christ
who face persecution in countries throughout the world through charities such as
Open Doors and Barnabas Fund. We also continue to respond throughout the year to
emergency appeals as a result of either man-made or natural disasters. A regular
Traidcraft stall is held at church at least once a month, promoting awareness of the
need for fair and equal trade with developing countries. Through MECO, we offer
support to the persecuted church.
(d) Pastoral work
Christchurch endeavours to offer a comprehensive pastoral support network to all
who have connections with Christchurch. Pastoral care, including visiting the sick and
bereaved, is led and overseen by the Ministry Team who in turn involve area and
individual pastoral visitors. House groups and other organizations that are under the
Christchurch umbrella also offer pastora care and support. Pastoral care is offered to
those at Archers Court, Imside and at The Cedars, properties associated with MHA
(formerly Methodist Homes for the Aged).
Open church, prayer station and outreach activities within the local community
provide companionship to those who wish to talk, be prayed for or be listened to.
Individual support is available to the bereaved.
Safeguarding is based on the Methodist Church policy "Safeguarding Children,
Young People and Vulnerable Adults". Our safeguarding group is responsible for
implementing this policy and ensuring that all users of our premises are aware of the
policy and its implications.
(e) Provision of facilities with a Christian ethos for the local community
Facilities with a Christian ethos provided for the local community include Open
Church: on 4 weekdays the church is open between 10.00 and 15.00, offering
hot/cold drinks and biscuits, as well as a listening ear and a quiet place to pray. It is
much appreciated by the homeless and many others.
There is a weekly Lunch Club; around 30 elderly people (referred by Social Services
or Age UK) are transported to the church, the majority by minibus, and provided with
activities and hospitality as well as lunch.
A Computer Centre is staffed by church members on 2 days per week offering
opportunity for anybody wishing to learn and practise skills at operating a computer.
The 'After School Club', run weekly by the Youth Worker and local Christian Schools'
Worker, offers to youngsters a range of activities and a place to chat.
(f) Support of other charities in the UK and overseas.
During the year a total of over f14,000 was donated to some 30 other charities in the
UK and places abroad including yria and the Philippines as well as other charities
working worldwide. f3,000 was donated from the church's general account, but the
bulk of the charitable giving was raised through donations and a variety of methods
including coffee mornings, concerts, charity quizzes, social activities and sponsored
events, organised by individuals and Groups associated with the church.
6
Activities shared with others
Christchurch also engages in a range of activities with others including the North
Herts Circuit of the Methodist church, the North Herts Group of United Reformed
Churches, and with Letchworth Free Church with whom it shares a minister.
Christchurch plays a prominent role in Churches Together in Hitchin which organises
events such as a Good Friday Walk of Witness, and communication of information
throughout the town about Christmas and Easter services at all constituent churches,
and also co-ordinates the annual house-to-house collection for Christian Aid.
With other churches in the town Christchurch, supports other community-led
initiatives including the North Herts Sanctuary and associated charities, helping the
Homeless and those then moving into accommodation.
More detail on the activities of the Charity can be found in the Christchurch Annual
Report, which is available on request.
Some statistics
Christchurch had 266 members as at 31 August 2014.
Average attendance at all services on a Sunday was 194, as measured during the
month of October 2012, in accordance with the procedures of the Methodist church.
This average consisted of 162 adults and 32 under 19s.
Maintenance of the Premises
Maintenance and cleaning of the premises at Christchurch are carried out and
monitored by the Finance & Property Committee and the Property Co-ordinator. A
Property Schedule has been prepared for forwarding to the Methodist
Superintendent, in accordance with Methodist Church procedures.
Apart from the completion of programmes already in place, no major works were
initiated in 2013-14 other than renewal of heating equipment. Some interior lighting
has been replaced in a number of rooms. Plans are also in place for painting of the
exterior timber and some classrooms, and for other electrical works.
During the past twelve months, approximately f40,000 has been spent on cleaning,
maintenance and repairs.
Risk Assessment
Each year, the Trustees carry out a Risk Assessment covering a range of risks,
including fire, accidents and other Health & Safety matters, finance and staff
shortage. Follow-up action is initiated as necessary.
Accounts
The Treasurer has prepared accounts for the year 2013-14, which have been
approved, have been audited by an Independent Examiner, and are attached (9
pages) to this report as submitted to the Methodist Church.
The Trustees have also formulated Accounting Policies and a Reserves Policy and
these are set out below:
7
Accounting policies
Incoming Resources
These are included in the Statement of Financial Activities (SOFA) when the church
becomes entitled to the resources, the Trustees are virtually certain they will receive
the resources and the monetary value can be measured with sufficient reliability.
Resources expended
Expenditure is recognised when a liability is incurred, or a constructive obligation
arises that results in the payment being unavoidable.
Liability recognition
Liabilities are recognized as soon as there is a legal or constructive obligation
committing the church to payout resources. Future income is not anticipated (in
accordance with SORP 2005).
Tangible fixed assets for use by church
Tangible fixed assets are capitalised if they can be used for more than one year, and
cost at least f1000. They are valued at cost or a reasonable value on receipt. Where
cost is not available, the Trustees have provided a reasonable estimate of cost or of
the buildings' current value to the church.
Reserves policy
It is the Trustees' policy to maintain a balance in reserve which equates to 6 months
of the assessments due to the Methodist and United Reformed Churches, and wages
for the Cleaner and Youth Worker, plus potential employment termination costs and
redundancy liabilities. In addition provision has been made for capital projects
expected to be carried out during the year.
Further details about the reserves policy are provided in the 2-page attachment
following the statement of accounts.

Notes to the Accounts
1 Basis of accounting
These accounts have been prepared on the basis of historic cost (except that investments are shown at market value) in accordance
with Statement of Recommended Practice (SORP 2005) and the Charities Act 2011. There has been no change to the accounting
policies since last year. The format of the SOFA balance sheet takes advantage of the smal charity concessions set out in the
SORP.
2 Funds
The funds held constitute: General Funds held for any purpose of the charity which are Unrestricted, Restricted funds which are held
for a narrower purpose and Endowment funds which represent gifts, the capital normally being unavailable for spending, and the
income from which is either Restricted or Unrestricted. Details of each material fund are disclosed in the fina Note to these Accounts.
Any fund may be held in ways other than in cash.
3 Accounting policies
Incoming Resources
These are included in the Statement of Financial Activities (SOFA) when the church becomes entitled to the resources, the trustees
are virtually certain they wil receive the resources, and the monetary value can be measured with sufficient reliability.
Resources Expended
Expenditure is recognised when a liability is incurred, or a constructive obligation arises that results in the payment being unavoidable.
Liability recognition
Liabilities are recognised as soon as there is a legal or constructive obligation committing the church to pay out resources. Future
income has not been anticipated (SORP 2005).
Tangible fixed assets for use by church
Tangible fixed assets are capitalised if they can be used for more than one year, and cost at least 21000. They are valued at cost or
a reasonable value on receipt. Where cost is not available, the Church Council may provide a reasonable estimate of cost or of the
buildings' current value to the church. An estimate based on insurance value should take account of the fact tha cover wil be based
on demolition and reinstating the current building and not the original cost less accumulated depreciation. A professiona valuation is
not required, but if available use that figure. Provision is made for depreciation where appropriate.
Investment properties
This includes Investment properties which are not used for the purposes of the charity (and never used for that purpose). A
professiona valuation is not required, but if available use that figure. The insured value may be used.
Investments
Investments are valued in the balance sheet at market value at the year end. Investment income is included in the accounts when
receivable and any gains or losses on revaluation at the year end are shown in the SOFA.
Include investments held with the Central Finance Board. Do not include Deposit Funds, such as TIF (Trustees Interest Fund) held
via Trustees for Methodist Church Purposes. These latter funds should be shown separately.
Debtors and Prepayments
These amounts are owed to the Church and include payments made by the Church for services yet to be received.
Creditors
These amounts are owed by the Church and include any amounts accrued as payable this year but wil be paid in future years.
